goal: Make GPU behavior visible using CUDA, Nsight, and PyTorch benchmarks.

tasks.title: Verify CUDA environment
tasks.objective: Confirm that NVIDIA driver, CUDA compiler, and PyTorch CUDA are working.
tasks.deliverable: Create hardware.md with GPU, driver, CUDA, PyTorch, CPU, RAM, and OS.

tasks.title: Run CUDA deviceQuery
tasks.objective: Inspect GPU hardware limits.
tasks.deliverable: Record compute capability, SM count, warp size, max threads per block, and global memory.

tasks.title: Run CUDA bandwidthTest
tasks.objective: Measure practical host-device and device memory bandwidth.
tasks.deliverable: Record measured memory bandwidth and compare it to theoretical GPU bandwidth if available.

tasks.title: Write first CUDA vector add kernel
tasks.objective: Implement C[i] = A[i] + B[i] and test different vector sizes/block sizes.
tasks.deliverable: Working kernel in labs/vector_add/vector_add.cu and a short note on achieved bandwidth.
tasks.reality_check: Why is vector add usually memory-bound? Answer in 2 sentences.

teaching_prompts.question: Why is vector add usually memory-bound? Answer in 2 sentences.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Good. Now: how would you change the kernel to be compute-bound instead? (one sentence is fine)
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Hint: think about the ratio of bytes read+written to FLOPs performed. A 1:1 element-wise op usually does very few FLOPs per element.

teaching_prompts.question: How would you make vector add faster without changing the algorithm?
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_match: Right. Vectorized loads + higher occupancy is the standard answer; the wall-clock win is usually 2-4x on a memory-bound kernel.
teaching_prompts.follow_up_if_miss: Two common answers: (a) load 4 floats at a time with float4 / vector loads, (b) increase occupancy so more in-flight loads hide the latency.

tasks.title: Profile with Nsight Systems
tasks.objective: Capture a timeline of the vector add kernel and identify gaps.
tasks.deliverable: Save the .nsys-rep and note kernel time vs total time.

tasks.title: Profile with Nsight Compute
tasks.objective: Get per-kernel metrics: SM occupancy, memory throughput, achieved bandwidth.
tasks.deliverable: Save ncu report and note achieved memory bandwidth vs roofline.
tasks.reality_check: What is the SOL (speed-of-light) percentage, and what does it tell you?

tasks.title: Run PyTorch matmul benchmark
tasks.objective: Benchmark matmul at multiple sizes and dtypes; see where TFLOPS plateau.
tasks.deliverable: Note TFLOPS at 1024, 4096, 8192 and the dtype that wins.

tasks.title: Write Week 1 report
tasks.objective: Synthesize all observations into gpu-week1-report.md.
tasks.deliverable: Complete gpu-week1-report.md with: hardware, key observations, bottlenecks found, and next steps.

tasks.title: Install Triton and run a sanity check
tasks.objective: Install Triton in a Python venv and run the bundled tutorial matmul kernel. Verify you can launch a Triton kernel on the available GPU.
commands: python -c "import triton; import triton.language as tl; print(triton.__version__)"
tasks.deliverable: notes/triton_setup.md with the installed Triton version, Python/PyTorch versions, and the GPU it ran on (or 'no NVIDIA GPU - documented what would have run').

tasks.title: First Triton kernel: vector add
tasks.objective: Re-implement the Week 1 vector add as a Triton kernel. Compare line count and readability vs the raw CUDA version.
tasks.deliverable: labs/vector_add/triton_vadd.py plus a 3-line note in reports/triton_vs_cuda.md comparing the two implementations.

tasks.title: Triton matmul with autotune
tasks.objective: Implement a block-tiled matmul in Triton and let autotune pick the best (BLOCK_M, BLOCK_N, BLOCK_K, num_stages) for your GPU.
tasks.deliverable: labs/triton_matmul/matmul.py and a note with the autotuned config + measured TFLOPS at one problem size.

tasks.title: Fused softmax: one kernel vs three
tasks.objective: Write a single-pass online softmax in Triton. Compare it to a naive three-pass softmax (max, subtract, exp+normalize) on a large row.
tasks.deliverable: labs/triton_softmax/softmax.py + a 5-line note on achieved bandwidth improvement.

tasks.title: Flash-attention-style kernel draft
tasks.objective: Draft a flash-attention-style block-tiled attention kernel. Goal is structure, not SOTA. Read the FlashAttention paper for the tiling idea.
tasks.deliverable: labs/triton_attention/attention.py (working or partially working) + a 1-paragraph note on what the FlashAttention paper does that a naive attention does not.

tasks.title: Triton vs raw CUDA synthesis
tasks.objective: Compare the Triton kernels from this week to the raw CUDA kernels from Week 1. Same workload, same machine. Note where Triton wins and where it loses.
tasks.deliverable: reports/triton_vs_cuda.md with a table of (kernel, lines-of-code, achieved perf, complexity of writing) for the vector_add and matmul comparisons.

tasks.title: Set up Python + transformers + a small model
tasks.objective: Install transformers + a small open model (e.g. Qwen2.5-0.5B or SmolLM2-360M) and verify you can load and run a forward pass.
commands: python -c "from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer; m = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B'); print(sum(p.numel() for p in m.parameters())/1e6, 'M params')"
tasks.deliverable: notes/llm_env.md with the model name, parameter count, device used (CPU / MPS / CUDA), and one sentence on what the model loaded successfully.

tasks.title: First generation: measure latency
tasks.objective: Run a single text generation, measure time-to-first-token (TTFT) and inter-token latency. Note the device and batch size.
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/first_gen.py + a 1-line note: 'TTFT=Xms, inter-token=Yms, device=Z'.

tasks.title: Profile prefill vs decode and watch KV cache grow
tasks.objective: Separate the prefill phase (processing the prompt) from the decode phase (one token at a time). Confirm the wall-clock asymmetry and watch the KV cache size scale with sequence length.
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/prefill_decode.py + a short note: 'prefill=Xms for N prompt tokens, decode=Yms per token, KV cache=ZMB at seq_len=W'.

tasks.title: Batched generation: throughput vs latency tradeoff
tasks.objective: Send B=1, 4, 16, 64 sequences through the model in a single batch. Plot tokens/sec vs batch size and per-request latency.
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/batch_sweep.py + a 3-line note on where throughput stops scaling (memory-bound? SM-bound?).
tasks.reality_check: At what batch size does throughput stop scaling? Why?

tasks.title: Int8 quantize a small model
tasks.objective: Apply int8 weight quantization (bitsandbytes or torchao) to the same small model. Measure latency, peak memory, and a tiny qualitative check (a few prompts, eyeball the output).
tasks.deliverable: labs/llm_bench/int8_quant.py + a 4-line note: latency delta, memory delta, qualitative output (pass/fail/almost).

tasks.title: LLM inference synthesis
tasks.objective: Synthesize this week's measurements into one report. Latency, memory, throughput, and what the bottleneck was at each scale.
tasks.deliverable: reports/llm_inference_week3.md with: prefill vs decode timing, KV cache growth curve, batch-sweep throughput curve, int8 quant tradeoffs.

tasks.title: Install vLLM (or TGI) and bring up a server
tasks.objective: Install a modern LLM serving engine (vLLM is the default pick). Start the OpenAI-compatible HTTP server and verify it responds.
tasks.deliverable: notes/serving_setup.md with the serving engine, model, port, and the curl command you used to verify it.

tasks.title: First HTTP request: TTFT under serving
tasks.objective: Send a single HTTP request to the server. Measure TTFT and TPOT under serving. Compare to the raw transformers latency from Week 3.
tasks.deliverable: notes/serving_first_request.md with TTFT/TPOT vs the Week 3 numbers. Was vLLM faster? By how much?
tasks.reality_check: Why does vLLM beat raw transformers on throughput even for a single request? (Hint: PagedAttention + continuous batching.)

tasks.title: Read the PagedAttention paper and the vLLM source
tasks.objective: Read the vLLM paper (or the Kwon et al. SOSP'23 paper). Skim the vLLM source for the KV cache manager. Write 5 bullets connecting the paper to the code.
tasks.deliverable: notes/paged_attention_notes.md - 5 bullets, each with one paper claim and one code location that backs it.

tasks.title: Streaming responses: TTFT vs TPOT tradeoff
tasks.objective: Switch the request to stream=true. Measure TTFT (time to first SSE chunk) and TPOT (inter-chunk time). Note the difference.
tasks.deliverable: notes/serving_streaming.md with TTFT and TPOT, and a 2-line note on why streaming improves perceived latency without reducing total time-to-last-token.

tasks.title: Concurrent requests: watch continuous batching kick in
tasks.objective: Send N=1, 4, 16, 64 concurrent requests with a small load tool. Watch throughput scale and per-request latency degrade. Confirm vLLM is continuous-batching (vs naive static batching).
tasks.deliverable: labs/serving_load/loadtest.py + a note: 'at N=X concurrent requests, aggregate throughput peaked at Y tok/s, per-request p99 latency was Zms'.

tasks.title: GPU serving synthesis
tasks.objective: Write the systems report: TTFT vs TPOT, streaming vs non-streaming, continuous batching at load, what the bottleneck was at each scale.
tasks.deliverable: reports/gpu_serving_week4.md with: latency curves, throughput curves, a paragraph on what PagedAttention buys you, and one sentence on what would break at 1000 concurrent requests.

tasks.title: Quantize a teacher model to int8 (PTQ) and measure the gap
tasks.objective: Take a small open model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / SmolLM2-360M), apply post-training int8 quantization (bitsandbytes or torchao), and measure latency, peak memory, and perplexity against the fp16 baseline. Quantize-aware-distillation is later; this task is just the baseline.
tasks.deliverable: notes/distill_quant_int8.md with the fp16 vs int8 latency, peak memory, and a 1-paragraph note on where the gap shows up in perplexity.
commands: python -c "from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM; m = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B', load_in_8bit=True, device_map='auto'); print(m.get_memory_footprint()/1e9, 'GB')"

tasks.title: LoRA fine-tune a small model on a domain corpus
tasks.objective: LoRA-fine-tune Qwen2.5-0.5B on a small domain corpus (e.g. 5k samples from a public dataset) using PEFT. Compare adapter weights to a full-fine-tune baseline in parameter count only.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_lora_report.md with adapter rank, target modules, training loss curve description, and a 1-paragraph note on what the adapter learned.
commands: python -c "from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model; print('peft ready')"

tasks.title: Train a student model to match a teacher (logit KD)
tasks.objective: Train a small student (e.g. SmolLM2-135M) to mimic a larger teacher (Qwen2.5-0.5B) by matching output logits. Use temperature-scaled KL divergence as the loss; freeze the teacher; train the student for a few hundred steps on a domain corpus.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_kd_report.md with loss curve, temperature, alpha, and a 1-paragraph note on where the student catches up to the teacher.

tasks.title: Evaluate teacher vs student vs LoRA vs int8 on a held-out task
tasks.objective: Build a small evaluation harness (perplexity on a held-out set, plus one downstream task from lm-evaluation-harness) and run it against the fp16 teacher, the int8 teacher, the LoRA-tuned teacher, and the distilled student. The point is the comparison, not the absolute number.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_eval.md with a 4-row table (model, perplexity, downstream task score, peak memory) and 2-3 sentences on the trade-offs.

tasks.title: Int4 quantize the distilled student (GPTQ or AWQ)
tasks.objective: Take the distilled student from the prior task and quantize it to int4 using GPTQ or AWQ. Measure latency, memory, and a tiny perplexity check. Compare against the int8 baseline.
tasks.deliverable: notes/distill_int4.md with method (GPTQ / AWQ), latency, memory, perplexity, and a 1-paragraph note on where int4 starts to hurt.
commands: pip install -U auto-gptq   # or: pip install -U awq

tasks.title: Distillation track synthesis: when does each method pay off?
tasks.objective: Write the synthesis for the distillation track. Pull together the int8 baseline, the LoRA fine-tune, the teacher-student training, the eval comparison, and the int4 quant into a single report. The point is the trade-off map: when does LoRA win, when does distillation win, when does int4 hurt too much, and what is the cost on a real Linux box vs Colab.
tasks.deliverable: reports/distill_synthesis.md with a 4-column trade-off table (method, cost-to-train, peak memory at inference, quality drop) and a 1-paragraph 'if you are on a Mac, do X' recommendation at the end.

cuda.title: CUDA Track
cuda.description: Kernels, profiling, memory hierarchy, and GPU execution.

llm.title: LLM Track
llm.description: Scaling laws, training dynamics, inference, and evaluation.

systems.title: Systems Track
systems.description: Inference pipelines, VAD, streaming, serving, and reliability.

distillation.title: Distillation Track
distillation.description: Compression, teacher-student training, quantization, and evaluation.

resources.title: Google Colab
resources.good_for: PyTorch, Transformers, small CUDA experiments, quick GPU time
resources.not_good_for: vLLM serving (long-running), anything needing persistent storage or >1 GPU
resources.notes: Use the GPU runtime. Free-tier availability varies; paid tier is reliable.

resources.title: Kaggle Notebooks
resources.good_for: Free notebook GPU, PyTorch, Transformers, small fine-tuning runs
resources.not_good_for: Anything needing internet at runtime, vLLM serving, anything that needs >1 GPU
resources.notes: 30h/week free GPU. Internet/package install limits apply.

resources.title: Unsloth
resources.good_for: LLM fine-tuning, LoRA, 4-bit / 8-bit quantization, runs in Colab
resources.not_good_for: Raw CUDA kernel work, anything that needs vLLM serving
resources.notes: Best fit for Week 3 quantization and any future distillation weeks. Not for Week 1/2 raw CUDA.

resources.title: RunPod
resources.good_for: Triton, vLLM, real Linux NVIDIA environment, persistent dev box, on-demand pricing
resources.not_good_for: Anything that needs zero setup (you are spinning up a VM)
resources.notes: Best match for Week 2-4 full labs. Per-hour billing; Community Cloud is cheap.

resources.title: Lambda Labs
resources.good_for: Persistent Linux GPU box, multi-GPU training, sustained workloads, full labs
resources.not_good_for: Anything you only need for an hour (use RunPod)
resources.notes: Reserved instances; per-month billing. Good when you know you want a real dev machine for a few weeks.
